answers.answer: C) The junior dev underestimated the complexity they removed - guide them to handle the lost edge cases within their simpler design. Reason: This is classic Dunning-Kruger effect - the junior dev did not realize the 'unnecessary abstractions' handled important edge cases (token refresh, session invalidation, rate limiting). Rejecting entirely (B) misses a teaching opportunity. Merging then fixing (D) creates technical debt. Best approach: guide them to understand why edge cases matter and help integrate those requirements into their simpler design.

V8 Engine Explanation with confidence ratings:

1. Parsing/AST [CERTAIN]: JS source parsed into tokens then AST.
2. Ignition [CERTAIN]: Interpreter converts AST to platform-independent bytecode, provides fast startup but slower execution.
3. TurboFan [PROBABLE]: Optimizing compiler converts hot bytecode to machine code using type feedback for aggressive optimizations.
4. Hidden classes [CERTAIN]: V8 creates hidden classes for objects; same-structure objects share class, enabling offset-based property access instead of hash tables.
5. Inline caching [PROBABLE]: Caches property lookup results; after first lookup, V8 remembers hidden class and offset for direct access.
6. Deoptimization [SPECULATIVE]: When optimization assumptions violated (e.g., type changes), V8 bails out from optimized code back to interpreter.

Self-assessment: CERTAIN=3, PROBABLE=2, SPECULATIVE=1, UNKNOWN=0. Reflection: I have decent high-level understanding of V8 architecture (interpreter + optimizing compiler) but lack depth in specific optimization techniques. My knowledge breadth is okay but depth needs improvement for serious JS performance optimization work.
